figures.caption: Table 4: Adaptive parameters used by EQUIMEM. Each is computed from observable round statistics rather than tuned.
Table 6: Key hyperparameters and configurations in EQUIMEM.
Figure 1: Failures of debate-driven memory commitment.

Figure 6: Adversarial robustness of LLM Audit (top row) and PPL Filter (bottom row) under Memory Poisoning (left column) and Audit Collusion (right column). Same setting as Figure 3: MacNet, Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct, N = 6, k = 1, 2, 3 adversaries. Dashed orange lines show each method’s benign performance (k = 0, from Table 1). Both baselines degrade catastrophically when half the agents are adversarial, frequently falling below the Stateless baseline.
Table 9: Performance with alternative LLM backbones (%) on MacNet, N = 6 agents, benign setting.

Table 8: Ablation study of EQUIMEM’s calibration components (%). We ablate one component at a time, keeping all notation consistent with Section 4. For embedding memory, ρdetect and ρalign are removed directly. For graph memory, we remove components contributing to ρdetect and ρalign, including an explicit ablation of ρalign. All configurations use AutoGen + Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct, N = 6 agents, benign setting (mean±std over 5 runs). Bold indicates the full EQUIMEM.

Table 10: Effect of agent count N on benign-setting performance (AutoGen, Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct, G-Memory backbone). Values are reported in percentage (mean±std over 5 runs). ∆ denotes the gain of EQUIMEM over the next-best safeguard under the same agent count.

Table 3: Per-component mapping between the three calibration quantities of EQUIMEM and the utility components they control at the calibrated equilibrium. Each row identifies the component, the calibration quantity that controls it, and the property that does the control.
Table 14: Summary of failure modes. All three are caused by data-level conditions, not by design limitations of Φ.

Table 5: Time complexity of each calibration stage and the ratio of added cost to baseline retrieval cost. The ratios are small under typical operating regimes: K ≪ k (delta size much smaller than top-k width), |At| a small constant, |Vt| a small graph, |E∆t | ≪ dLmax/2 (incremental updates). Under these conditions every added stage is sub-baseline, and total calibration overhead remains marginal relative to one round of baseline retrieval.

Table 1: Performance comparison with baseline memory safeguarding or calibration approaches. We report accuracy for HotpotQA (HQA), F1 for LoCoMo (LCM), success rate for ALFWorld (ALF), and reward for WebShop (WS) (mean±std over 5 runs). Note that A-MemGuard [78] applies only to

Figure 5: LoCoMo F1 vs. debate rounds on two memory architectures (MacNet, Qwen3-VL-8BInstruct, N = 6). Round 5 values match Table 1. MemBank (left) saturates after round 3; G-Memory (right) improves steadily. EQUIMEM follows the same trend shape as Vanilla in both cases but at a consistently higher level, with the gap widening at later rounds.
Figure 4: Performance vs. token consumption. Each sub-figure shows MemBank (M) and G-Memory (G) under five calibration methods. Bubble size reflects token-cost variability.

Figure 3: Adversarial robustness of EQUIMEM under two attacks. Bars show performance at k = 1, 2, 3 adversarial agents; dashed orange lines show benign performance (k = 0). Dots show individual run results (5 seeds). Baseline comparisons are in App. D.8.
